perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, without reference to conflict of laws principles. This docu-ment contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. Any failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver thereof or of any other provision hereof. This Agreement may not be amended, nor any obligation waived, except by a writing signed by both parties hereto. Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, without reference to conflict of laws principles. This docu-ment contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof. Any failure to enforce any provision of this Agreement shall not constitute a waiver thereof or of any other provision hereof. Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts. This Agreement may not be amended, nor any obligation waived, except by a writing signed by both parties hereto.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit statement that 'Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts,' and placing it elsewhere the enforceability regarding jurisdiction is weakened.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the explicit venue selection can be seen as a contradiction because while Massachusetts law might govern the contract, the *location* of a trial can still be challenged based on convenience of parties and witnesses under 28 U.S.C. § 1404, potentially undermining the original intent of exclusive jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The original contract specified that disputes would be adjudicated exclusively in Massachusetts. The changed text removes this clause. According to 28 U.S.C. § 1404 (stated in both scraped snippets), a district court may transfer any civil action to another district for the convenience of parties and witnesses, potentially undermining the original agreement to adjudicate disputes exclusively in Massachusetts. The original clause aimed to limit venue options, and its removal directly contradicts the ability to enforce that limitation, as the case could be moved elsewhere.

perturbation.original_text: ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. NEITHER PARTY MAKES ANY WARRANTIES, EXPRESS, IMPLIED OR OTHERWISE, REGARDING ITS ACCURACY, COMPLETENESS OR PERFORMANCE.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unau- thorized use of the Confidential Information. ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION IS PROVIDED “AS IS”. Without limiting the foregoing, Receiving Party shall take at least those measures that Receiving Party takes to protect its own most highly confidential information and shall have its employees, if any, who have access to Confidential Information sign a nonuse and non-disclosure agreement in content substantially similar to the provi-sions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'AS IS' warranty disclaimer into the section detailing the receiving party's obligations weakens its enforceability as a blanket disclaimer. By embedding it within the obligations clause, it can be argued that the disclaimer only applies to the Receiving Party's actions, not the Disclosing Party's representations.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 dictates how warranty disclaimers should be conspicuous and unambiguous. Placing the 'AS IS' statement within the obligations paragraph makes it less conspicuous and potentially ambiguous, as it might be interpreted to only relate to the Receiving Party's confidentiality obligations, rather than a general disclaimer of warranties on the information itself.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 2-316 regarding the conspicuousness of warranty disclaimers. By placing the 'AS IS' disclaimer within a paragraph detailing the receiving party's obligations concerning confidential information, the change makes the disclaimer less conspicuous and potentially ambiguous. This could lead to a court interpreting the disclaimer as only applying to the Receiving Party's actions related to confidentiality rather than a general disclaimer of warranties. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (3)(a), highlights that 'as is' language excludes implied warranties, but the placement impacts whether it 'calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty.' Here, the placement obscures the disclaimer's broad applicability, thus creating a legal contradiction. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction', where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section.
